I have heard the learned counsel for the parties at length and have carefuily perused the record, includir-rg the irrtpugncd Juclgment and Decree, the Order of the learned u'ial Coirrt' an'-l the plaint filed by the Applicants/Plaintiffs' The primary question before this Court is whether the Iearned Courts Lrelow were justified in reiecting the plaint under Order ViI 'Rule 11 CPC. Older Mi Ruie 11 CPC provides for the rejection of a (a) Where it does not disclose a cause ot action' (b) Wrere the relief claimed is undervalued' hnd the plaintiff fails to correct the valuation' l (c) Where the plaint is written on PaPer insufficiently stamped, and the plaintiff fails to supply the requisittr stamp PaPer. (d) Where the suit aPPears from the statemertt in the plaint to be barred bY anY law. It is a settled principle of law that while consi{ering an application under Ordcr VII Rule 11 CPC' the Court must I confine itseU to the avelments made in the plaint and the A. NO. 172 0F 2021 5 Iaint in the following cases: (r: IQ F o J ttc U * I ?/7.A. NO. 1?2 0F 2021'6 )- documents filed along with it. The Court cannot consider tire ciefense taken by the defendant.

NO. 1?2 0F 2021'6 )- documents filed along with it. The Court cannot consider tire ciefense taken by the defendant. The plaint can be reiected orrly if the averments made theleiry even if taken to be true, do not ciisclose a cause of action or if the suit appears to be baned by any 1aw. In the present case, the leained trial Cour; reiected drc plaint primarily on the ground that it was barred by iimitatiou' The Trial Court noted that the alleged occupation of the Ianc'l occurred in 1976, and the suit was filed in 2018, more than 40 years 1ater. The Trial Court further noted that tire Applicants/Plaintiffs were minors n 1976 but had attained majority several years before filing the suit. The Tlial Court held that the suit was barrecl by the Law of Limitation' Tlre learned Appellate Court upheld the trial Court's decision' agreeing that the suit was barretl by limitation' The Appellate Court also noted that the title documents favored the Defendant (Respondent) No. l and that the transfer ha..1 occurred decades ago. After careful consideration, I arn of the view that the iearned Courts below have correctly applied the Hu- law and have rightly reiected the plaint under Order VII Rule 11 CPC. The suit filed by the Applicants/Plaintiffs is essentially for declaration of their title to the suit land, possession' and mesne profits. f,he alleged cause of action arose in 1976 when H.E. Shaikh Zayed'Btn Sultan Al-Nahyan allegedly occupietl * F t! 'n o C.1 S* \\ the land. Even if the Applicants,/Plaintiffs were minors at that time, they attained majority several years before filing 'the suit' Article 120 of the Limitation Act, 1908, establishes a six-ye4r limitation period for filing a suit seeking a declaration when no specific.time frame is provided elsewhere in the Scheciule' Additionally, Article 142 stipulates a twelve-year limitation periocl for suits concerning the possession of immov'able property, commencing from the date of dispossession rtr discontinuarce of possession.

Shaikh Zayed'Btn Sultan Al-Nahyan allegedly occupietl * F t! 'n o C.1 S* \\ the land. Even if the Applicants,/Plaintiffs were minors at that time, they attained majority several years before filing 'the suit' Article 120 of the Limitation Act, 1908, establishes a six-ye4r limitation period for filing a suit seeking a declaration when no specific.time frame is provided elsewhere in the Scheciule' Additionally, Article 142 stipulates a twelve-year limitation periocl for suits concerning the possession of immov'able property, commencing from the date of dispossession rtr discontinuarce of possession. In the present case, the suit rtas institutecl more than 30 years after the Applicants/Plaintiffs 2rz A. NO. 172 0F 2021 7 hacl reached the age o{ majority and over 40 years after the alleged occupation of the land. Consequently' the suit;is evidently time-barred. The contention raised by the learned counsel for the Applicants/Plaintiffs, asserting that frauduient entries made in the revenue records in 2011 constitutecl a fresh cause of action, is untenable. The Applicants/Piaintitfs were already aware of the alleged occupation of the land sit)ce 7976' and atw flaudulent entries, if proven, woulcl not extend the limitation period applicabie to the origtral cause of acUion' The llower Courts have correctly determined that the plaint fiailed to ciisclose a valid cause of action and that the suit was barred !1' limitation. Therefore, the rejection of tire plaint under Order VII Rule 11 of the Code of Civil Procedure (CPC) whs fully justified, 8. It has also been observed that since the Applicants' title was disputed and placed under doubt, they were requirec{ tb seek a declaration of ownership before claiming possession ancl permanent injunction' Without such a declaratory relief' tlrc grant of these remedies is not tegally permissible' as es{ablishcd by the Apex Cor:rt of Pakistan irrProainca of the Punia'b zr',; Ghazanfar Ati Shah (2077 SCMR 772), Sultan Mehmo,ocl Shah;' o. Din Mtthammail (2005 SCMR 7872), andMrthammad Aslam a.Ferozi@LD20075c213).Furthermore.sincetheAPplicants also sought the cancellation of Entry No'41 in the record of rights, it was mandatory for them to file a declaratory suit against the Respondent No'1, as required under Section 53 of the Land Revenue Act, '1967 ' Their failure to seek such tr cleclaration in accordance with the statutory provisions rentlets their claim legallY deficient' GHC c) 14 * { o F t!. g.

The scope of revisional iurisdiction under Sechon 115 of the Code of Civil Procedure is limited' The High Court can 3q ft.A. NO. 172 0r 2021 r interfere only if the subordinate court has: (a) exet'cised'a jurisdiction not vested in it by law; or (b) faiied to o(ercis€ a jurisdiction so vestecl; or (c) acted in the exercise of lits jurisdiction illegatly or with material irregularitv The Applicants have not been able to demonstrate any such jurisdictional' error or material irregularity' The lower Courts had the iurisdiction to entertain and decicle the sujr' 'rnt1 thev exercised that jurisdiction in accordance with the lait" l'l-rc mcie fact that the Applicants disagree with the findings of the lower courts does not constitute a ground for interference in levision' It is a well-established principle that a revisional couft' wiri]9 exercising iurisdiction under Section 115 of the C'P'C'' generally does not interfere with concurrent findings of fact recc''rdcd hv the two Courts below' This principle is based on the:prcmise that an appellate court serves as the final authoriW 'for determining disputecl questions of fact' However' this r'ule is Hc not absolute. There are exceptional circumstanccs where terventionunderSectionll5oftheC'P'C'mayber'r'arranted' tA,!t', t uch as in cases of gross misreading or non-reading of Eviclenqe o have exercised their on record, or when the courts below s t jurisdiction illegally or with material irregularity' In this regard' reliance maY be Supreme Court placecl on the dictum laid dou'ir by the of Pakistan in|.aii Waidad o' Proainciql 'G oaernment Through Secretary Board of Reaerue Gotternment of Balochistan, Quetta and otherc (2020 SCMR 2046) It is a matterofrecordthattheApplicanthasrrotonlyfailedto clemonstrate gross misreading, non-reading of er''ii'ence' illegality, or material irregularity but has also been unable to establish any exceptional circumstances warla4ti4g intervention iri the concurrent findings of fact recordcd by the learned Courts below.

For the reasons stated above, this Court finds no merif in the present Civil Revision Application' The Applicants fave failed to establish any jurisdictional error or sigr'ificant irregularify in tlte impugned ' judgments ancl decler:s oi the lower Courts.
